perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to instruct all such employees that they may not use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Business Purpose and (except as permitted by the terms of this Agreement) not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties, including consultants, without the prior written consent of Premisys.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient should endeavor to instruct its employees that they avoid using Confidential Information for purposes beyond the Business Purpose, and generally refrain from disclosing such Confidential Information to third parties, including consultants, absent explicit consent from Premisys.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'agrees to instruct' with 'should endeavor to instruct', weakening the obligation. 'May not use' is replaced with 'avoid using,' and 'not to disclose' with 'generally refrain from disclosing,' both making the restrictions less mandatory and more discretionary. The removal of 'prior written' before consent also introduces ambiguity. This potentially violates the requirement for clear and enforceable confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text weakens the obligation to ensure employees maintain confidentiality, which could be interpreted as failing to take reasonable measures, thereby jeopardizing trade secret protection under California law. By changing the language from a strict obligation to an encouraged practice, the agreement potentially undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially conflicting with CUTSA's requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to protect trade secrets, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text uses mandatory language ('agrees to instruct,' 'may not use,' 'not to disclose'), while the changed text uses weaker, discretionary language ('should endeavor to instruct,' 'avoid using,' 'generally refrain from disclosing'). This shift from mandatory to discretionary could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures to maintain secrecy, as required to protect trade secrets under California law, specifically CIV 3426.1, which addresses 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Recipient or any of its Representatives is requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the Confidential Information of Premisys, Recipient shall provide Premisys with prompt written notice of any such request or requirement so that Premisys may seek a protective order, confidential treatment or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Recipient or any of its Representatives is requested or required to disclose any of the Confidential Information of Premisys, Recipient will consider providing Premisys with notice of such request or requirement, allowing Premisys the option to seek a protective order or other remedy.
perturbation.explanation: The changes significantly weaken the obligation. 'Shall provide' becomes 'will consider providing,' making the notice discretionary. 'Prompt written notice' is removed, and the purpose of the notice ('so that Premisys may seek a protective order') is softened to 'allowing Premisys the option'. This contradicts the legal duty to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section allows for protective orders to protect confidential information during discovery. By weakening the requirement for prompt notice to Premisys, the modified text impedes Premisys's ability to seek a protective order in a timely manner, thereby potentially violating the spirit and intent of the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2017.010 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 2. Scope of Discovery [2017.010 - 2017.320] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) ARTICLE 1. General Provisions [2017.010 - 2017.020] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2017.010. Unless otherwise limited by order of the court in accordance with this title, any party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action or to the determination of any motion made in that action, if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Discovery may relate to the claim or defense of the party seeking discovery or of any other party to the action. Discovery may be obtained of the identity and location of persons having knowledge of any discoverable matter, as well as of the existence, description, nature, custody, condition, and location of any document, electronically stored information, tangible thing, or land or other property. (Amended by Stats. 2012, Ch
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original contract and undermines the protection of confidential information. The original text mandates 'prompt written notice,' whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the recipient 'will consider providing' notice, making it discretionary. The removal of 'prompt written notice' and softening of the action Premisys can take impede Premisys's ability to seek a protective order in a timely manner. This directly contradicts the duty to protect confidential information that protective orders provide, as stated in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly mention confidential information, it allows parties to obtain discovery regarding any matter, which implies that protective orders might be necessary to safeguard privileged or confidential information. The modification creates an Ambiguous Legal Obligation, leading to a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of a Termination Notice, Recipient shall, within twenty (20) calendar days, either: (i) return the Confidential Materials in its possession to Premisys and instruct its Representatives to do the same; (ii) destroy the Confidential Materials in a secure manner and instruct its Representatives to do the same; or (iii) at Premisys' election, take any combination of steps (i) and (ii).
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of a Termination Notice, Recipient will make reasonable efforts to, within approximately twenty (20) calendar days, return or destroy the Confidential Materials. Recipient will also encourage its Representatives to do the same.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the mandatory obligation to return or destroy confidential materials. 'Shall' becomes 'will make reasonable efforts to,' introducing a subjective standard. 'Twenty (20) calendar days' is softened to 'approximately twenty (20) calendar days,' creating uncertainty. 'Instruct its representatives to do the same' is changed to 'encourage its Representatives to do the same' which is not mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA focuses on misappropriation, the 'reasonable efforts' standard undermines the obligation to protect trade secrets. If the recipient fails to return or destroy the information due to a vaguely defined 'reasonable effort', this could lead to potential misappropriation (or the risk thereof) which could have been avoided had the original contractual terms remained strict and fully enforced.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the legal obligation to protect trade secrets. The original contract mandates specific actions (return or destroy confidential materials), while the changed text introduces a 'reasonable efforts' standard, weakening the obligation. Snippet 1, referencing California Code, CIV 3426.2, allows for injunctions against 'actual or threatened misappropriation'. By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation, potentially violating the intent of the law. The original strict obligation, if enforced, prevents the possible misappropriation of trade secrets, while the relaxed standard in the modified text creates a higher chance of misappropriation, contradicting the protections intended in CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Beginning on Effective Date and continuing for a period of six months thereafter, Recipient will not, either for itself or for any other person or entity, directly or indirectly, solicit, induce or attempt to induce any employee of Premisys to terminate his or her employment with Premisys.
perturbation.changed_text: Beginning on the Effective Date and continuing for about six months thereafter, Recipient will generally avoid directly soliciting employees of Premisys to terminate their employment.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains a clear non-solicitation clause. The change introduces ambiguity by replacing 'will not solicit, induce or attempt to induce' with 'will generally avoid directly soliciting'. 'About six months' is less specific than 'a period of six months'. The change weakens the protection against employee poaching.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements, the modified non-solicitation clause, by being vague and less restrictive ('generally avoid directly soliciting'), might be interpreted as an unenforceable restraint of trade if it effectively prevents employees from freely leaving and joining other companies. The phrase 'generally avoid' is not sufficiently defined, leading to potential disputes about what constitutes a violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction because it weakens the original non-solicitation clause. The original text strictly prohibits solicitation, while the changed text states the recipient will 'generally avoid directly soliciting,' which is ambiguous and less restrictive. This change undermines the protection against employee poaching. According to the Law Explanation, this vagueness might be interpreted as an unenforceable restraint of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' Although the original clause could be argued as a restraint of trade, the modified version, by being vague, introduces ambiguity about its enforceability and scope, potentially still creating a restraint of trade but in a less clear way. By weakening the original clause into a more ambiguous one, creates a legal contradiction, creating difficulty to enforce under state or national law, and leaving legal obligations open to dispute.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties hereby irrevocably consents to the exclusive jurisdiction of and venue in the United States District Court for the Northern District of California in connection with any litigation of a dispute between them arising from or relating to this Agreement and waives any and all right to object to the jurisdiction of such court or to claim that venue in such court is not proper.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties generally agree to the jurisdiction of the United States District Court for the Northern District of California for disputes related to this Agreement, and will consider waiving objections to such jurisdiction where appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes exclusive jurisdiction and venue in a specific court. The modified text weakens this by replacing 'irrevocably consents to the exclusive jurisdiction' with 'generally agree to the jurisdiction'. It also changes the waiver of objections from absolute ('waives any and all right') to conditional ('will consider waiving objections where appropriate'). This introduces uncertainty and potential challenges to enforcing the agreement in the specified court.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1391 outlines the general rules for venue in federal courts. While parties can contractually agree to venue, the modified clause's ambiguity undermines the certainty and enforceability of such an agreement. By using phrases like 'generally agree' and 'will consider waiving,' the clause creates an opening for a party to later argue that venue is improper under § 1391, thus complicating and potentially prolonging litigation. By adding the element of optionality, the certainty guaranteed by the original jurisdiction clause is lost.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates exclusive jurisdiction in a specific court, waiving all objections. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating parties 'generally agree' to jurisdiction and 'will consider waiving' objections. This undermines the certainty of the original agreement. 28 U.S.C. § 1391 governs venue, and while parties can agree on venue contractually, the changed text weakens this agreement. The law snippets, specifically mentioning 'Venue generally' and that 'this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States', supports that the modification creates ambiguity that could allow a party to challenge venue based on § 1391, contradicting the original intent of exclusive jurisdiction.
